skill.name: git-workflow
skill.description: Guide Git work from conventional commits through branch naming, verification, commit creation, and push readiness.
skill.summary: Standardize Git commits, branches, and pre-completion checks for repository work.
skill.author: Doyajin174

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command and weak cryptography matches in SKILL.md. Review found no prompt injection, network access, credential access, or malicious intent; the weak cryptography matches are false positives from placeholders such as description text. The real risk is that the skill recommends Bash, Git, npm, and npx commands, including repository-defined npm scripts.

medium_findings.title: Repository Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run npm test, npm run lint, npx tsc, npm run build, git add, git commit, and git push. These commands are legitimate for a Git workflow skill, but npm scripts and push operations can execute repository-defined code or alter remote repositories if used without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly present in Bash examples and are semantically relevant to the skill. Risk is operational rather than malicious because no evidence shows obfuscation, exfiltration, or unauthorized network behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Matches
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography findings map to description placeholders and conventional commit examples, not cryptographic algorithms. No evidence found of DES, MD5, SHA-1, custom encryption, or credential handling in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirms these lines contain metadata, placeholders, branch names, or commit message examples. There is no cryptographic code or instruction on these lines.

low_findings.title: False Positive Ruby Backtick Matches
low_findings.description: The medium-severity Ruby backtick findings are Markdown inline code spans or fenced examples, not Ruby shell execution. No evidence found of executable Ruby code or dynamic command construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is documentation with Markdown code fences and inline code. The detected backticks do not execute commands and are not part of a script file.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains Bash command examples for testing, linting, type checking, building, committing, and pushing changes. Users should review commands and repository scripts before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and relevant to the workflow. They are not malicious by themselves, but they can cause filesystem, test, build, and remote Git side effects.

content.value_statement: Git work often fails because commits, branches, and final checks are inconsistent. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a clear workflow for commits, verification, and push readiness.
actual_capabilities: Defines conventional commit formats with common commit types and breaking change examples.
Lists pre-completion checks for tests, linting, TypeScript validation, and builds.
Provides a simple branch strategy and branch naming examples.
Outlines a commit workflow from staging files to pushing changes.
Supplies checklists for commit quality and completion verification.
limitations: It does not inspect repository history or enforce Git policy by itself.
It assumes npm-based verification commands and may need adaptation for other stacks.
It does not define rollback, release tagging, or pull request review rules.
It may run commands with side effects when an agent is allowed to use Bash.

use_cases.title: Prepare clean commits
use_cases.description: A developer can ask an agent to organize changed files and draft a conventional commit message.

use_cases.title: Verify work before handoff
use_cases.description: A team lead can require tests, linting, type checks, and builds before a task is marked complete.

use_cases.title: Teach consistent Git habits
use_cases.description: A new contributor can follow branch naming, commit format, and final checklist guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a commit message
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my current changes and suggest a conventional commit message with the correct type and scope.

prompt_templates.title: Run completion checks
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Git workflow skill to verify this task before completion. Report test, lint, type check, and build status.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a branch workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Recommend a branch name and commit plan for this feature. Use the branch strategy and conventional commit rules.

prompt_templates.title: Complete commit and push review
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit my staged and unstaged changes, confirm verification status, draft the commit message, and identify any risks before pushing.

output_examples.input: Review my changes and suggest a commit message.
output_examples.output: Suggested commit: feat(auth): add JWT login. Rationale: the change adds authentication behavior and fits the feat type.

output_examples.input: Check whether this task is ready to finish.
output: Remaining issue: remove temporary console logging before commit

output_examples.input: Create a branch name for fixing issue 42 in the API parser.
output_examples.output: Recommended branch: fix/42-api-parser. It follows the documented fix branch pattern and includes the issue number.

best_practices: Review each command before execution, especially npm scripts and git push.
Run verification commands before declaring work complete or asking for review.
Use specific scopes in commit messages when the repository structure makes them clear.
anti_patterns: Do not commit unrelated changes just because they are present in the working tree.
Do not push without confirming the target branch and remote repository.
Do not skip tests or lint checks unless the reason is documented.

faq.question: Does this skill run Git commands automatically?
faq.answer: It provides workflow guidance and command examples. Actual execution depends on the host agent permissions and user approval model.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: It does not request network access beyond normal Git push guidance. Review all commands before execution in private repositories.

faq.question: Can it enforce conventional commits?
faq.answer: It explains the format and suggests messages. It does not install hooks or enforce policy by itself.

faq.question: Does it support non-npm projects?
faq.answer: The documented checks use npm and TypeScript examples. Other ecosystems need adjusted test, lint, and build commands.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill recommends external commands. They are legitimate, but npm scripts and Git push can have important side effects.

faq.question: Does the skill contain prompt injection text?
faq.answer: No evidence found in SKILL.md. The reviewed content is standard Git workflow documentation.
